title: The Digital Memory We Cannot Afford to Lose: Knowledge Rights 21 Signs the Our Future Memory Statement
content:
Knowledge Rights 21 (KR21) has signed the Our Future Memory Statement on the Four Digital Rights of Memory Institutions. We join the International Federation of Library Associations and Institutions (IFLA), the International Council on Archives (ICA), the Wikimedia Foundation, and hundreds of libraries, archives, and museums across six continents in calling on policymakers to ensure that memory institutions retain online the same rights they have always held offline: the right to collect, to preserve, to provide access, and to cooperate across borders.

The timing is not coincidental. The urgency is real.

In May 2026, Mark Graham, the director of the Wayback Machine, described it as “collateral damage caught up in the conflict between AI companies and publishers.” News publishers, including some of the world’s most prominent titles, have begun blocking the Internet Archive from archiving their websites. The stated reason is concerns about AI scraping. The effect is that the public record of those publications, part of our collective memory, is no longer being preserved.
snippet: Knowledge Rights 21 (KR21) has signed the Our Future Memory Statement on the Four Digital Rights of Memory Institutions. We join the International Federation of Library Associations and Institutions
creator: KR21
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: Sofia University Library Awarded “Library of the Year 2025” in Bulgaria for Leadership in Open Science
content:
Blog post by Ana Lazarova

On May 14, 2026, the Sofia University Library “St. Kliment Ohridski” – the academic library of Bulgaria’s oldest and largest university – received Bulgaria’s Library of the Year 2025 award. The distinction was presented at the XVI Annual Library Awards ceremony, organised by the Bulgarian Library and Information Association during the XX National Library Week. The University library was nominated by the Academic Council of Sofia University in recognition of its achievements in open science and digitisation.

The main reason for the nomination and the award was the library’s active implementation of the secondary publication mechanisms adopted at the national level in recent years.

In late 2023, Bulgaria adopted a Secondary Publishing Right (SPR) into its copyright legislation – Article 60, paragraphs 2 and following of the Copyright and Neighbouring Rights Act. This created an imperative regime preventing publishers from overriding specific author reuse rights.
creator: KR21 Guest
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: National Workshop on Public Lending Right: Strengthening Moldova’s Path Toward EU Integration
content:
Blog post by Mariana Harjevschi

On 23 and 24  April 2026, coinciding with World Book and Copyright Day, the National Workshop on Public Lending Right (PLR) took place in Chișinău in a hybrid format. The event brought together policymakers, international experts, and representatives of libraries, authors’ organisations, and cultural institutions to advance discussions on introducing Public Lending Right in the Republic of Moldova.

The workshop was organised by the State Agency on Intellectual Property (AGEPI) with the support of the EU4IP project and Swiss Partnership Moldova. It formed part of the broader national effort to align copyright legislation with the EU acquis and to explore sustainable policy options for implementing PLR.

The opening session featured contributions from institutional and international partners, highlighting the importance of coordinated action between government bodies, libraries, and the cultural sector. Discussions underscored that the implementation of PLR requires not only legal adjustments but also institutional readiness and sustainable financing mechanisms.
creator: KR21 Guest
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: 10 Takeaways From the Twin EU Consultations About the Landscape for Copyright Reform
content:
In the past weeks, we have seen the launch of two consultations relating to the European Union’s legislation about copyright, and how it affects the ability of researchers, educators, learners and the libraries that support them to work.

The results of these will, a priori, play an important role in shaping the direction of travel of the European Union’s future work in this area, and in particular, potential legislation next year (tentatively titled ‘Directive enhancing the copyright environment for European creativity and innovation’).

The first consultation is an open Call for Evidence (25 June deadline) on what is needed in order to create ‘a better European copyright environment for European creativity and innovation’, allowing for open feedback but with a number of themes highlighted as being of particular interest.

title: Cyprus Event Report: Copyright and Access in the Age of AI
content:
Blog post by Ypapanti (Dia) Kytta | CALIS

Greek version available here

On 11 May 2026, Knowledge Rights 21, the Foundation for Parliamentarism and Participatory Democracy of the House of Representatives of the Republic of Cyprus, and the Cyprus Association of Librarians and Information Scientists (CALIS) co-organised an event in Nicosia entitled “Copyright and Access in the Age of Artificial Intelligence.” The event focused on the challenges posed by the rapid development of AI for knowledge, cultural heritage, and rights of access to information, with particular emphasis on the Cypriot context.

The event brought together representatives from libraries, universities, research and cultural institutions, members of governmental committees on AI and the European Union, and legal experts specialising in intellectual property law.
snippet:
Blog post by Ypapanti (Dia) Kytta | CALIS

Greek version available here

title: Cyprus Copyright Law, Research, and Education: A System Rich in Ambition, but Strained by Its Own Architecture
content:
Blog post by Professor Philippe Jougleux

Copyright law rarely attracts public attention unless a dispute reaches the courts or a controversy emerges around technology. Yet, for universities, libraries, researchers, teachers, and students, copyright rules shape everyday academic life. They determine what may be shared in a classroom, what can be digitised by a library, how research outputs circulate, and whether innovation is facilitated or delayed.

However, in this aspect, Cyprus is trying to do several demanding things at once. It wants to support research, teaching, libraries, cultural heritage, and open access. It also wants to remain attractive to investors, creators, and higher education institutions. The problem, as a new report supported by Knowledge Rights 21 sets out, is that the legal framework intended to serve all those goals has become difficult to navigate, uneven in practice, and at times almost symbolic rather than operational.

Author Philippe Jougleux, from the European University Cyprus, draws a twofold central lesson:

  • Cyprus’s lack of adequate copyright rules for research and education is not on par with its general ambition to become a hub for higher education.
snippet: Blog post by Professor Philippe Jougleux
creator: KR21 Guest
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: World IP Day 2026: Uneven Performance or Team Europe?
content:
Download KR21’s infographic

A key justification for intellectual property rules is their contribution to innovation. The prevailing logic suggests that the creation of (time-limited) monopolies allows creators to recoup the costs of developing new ideas.

This argument is relatively straightforward for measures such as patents, trademarks, or design rights (although concerns about abuses persist). 

However, it is more complicated regarding copyright. This doesn’t just regulate the creative industries, but equally affects citizens, scientists and researchers. The research process is explicitly about accessing and building on existing facts and insights. As such, anything that causes barriers to, or exclusion from, this needs to be strictly evaluated. Copyright needs to be designed with this objective in mind. 

Picking up (tangentially) on the theme of this year’s World Intellectual Property Day – IP and sport – we’re looking at who scores highest and lowest across the EU in ensuring that its copyright laws are on the side of research and innovation. But more importantly, we look at the unevenness of approaches, which undermines Europe’s ability to compete on the global stage.
creator: Stephen Wyber
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: Every Time a Library Cannot Do Its Job, the Public Interest Loses: Tell Us Your Story
content:
The mission of libraries is under threat. Not from budget cuts or censorship alone, but from a quieter crisis: a legal and market framework that is systematically limiting libraries’ ability to function. Publishers can refuse to license digital materials. Contracts can override rights granted by law. Digital locks can make legal activities impossible. Copyright rules written for classrooms leave libraries out. The result is the same every time: a user who needed something, and a library that could not provide it.

Knowledge Rights 21 (KR21) named these barriers in its action plan, Safeguard Access, Empower Europe: An Action Plan to Let Libraries be Libraries, launched in February 2026. Today, 23 April, on UNESCO’s World Book and Copyright Day, we are taking the next step.

Evidence matters in policy. Legislators and regulators need to understand not just that these problems exist in theory, but that they are happening in real libraries, to real users, right now. That is why KR21 is launching a case study and resource repository on the Library Action Plan page, seeded with initial examples covering each of the barriers the action plan identifies.

title: Moldova Symposium Report: Advocating to Let Libraries Be Libraries
content:
Blog post by Mariana Harjevschi

On 25 March 2026, Mariana Harjevschi, KR21 national coordinator for Moldova, presented the KR21 roadmap “Safeguard Access, Empower Europe: An Action Plan to Let Libraries be Libraries” at the 35th National Symposium “The Library Year 2025”, held in Chișinău. See the recording of Mariana’s intervention.

The symposium was organised by the National Library of the Republic of Moldova under the auspices of the Ministry of Culture. The theme of this year’s edition, “The Involvement of Libraries in Digital Transformation: Opportunities and Challenges”, reflects the strategic direction of 2025 and highlights the essential role of libraries in supporting the digital transformation of society.

Addressing fellow library professionals, Mariana emphasised that the mission of libraries is increasingly under threat, not only from budget constraints or censorship, but also from a “silent crisis” driven by legal and market frameworks that risk excluding libraries from the digital age. The KR21 library Action Plan sets out nine key actions to ensure that libraries across Europe can continue to support research, education, preservation, and cultural participation.
creator: KR21 Guest
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: Different Players, Same Game: What Libraries Have in Common With Gamers
content:
The European Citizens’ Initiative (ECI) on the right to resell digital games is on the agenda of a joint hearing by the European Parliament’s Internal Market and Consumer Protection (IMCO), Legal Affairs (JURI), and Petitions (PETI) Committee on 16 April.

The ECI addresses a practice that many consumers know well: buying a game on the understanding that they own it, only to find it switched off or deleted by the company that sold it. Knowledge Rights 21 (KR21) supports the push for action, and we have a direct interest in its outcome, because the problem the ECI identifies is not specific to games.

Libraries and the researchers, educators, learners, and citizens who depend on them operate under conditions that are structurally identical to those the ECI describes. Licensed digital collections disappear without warning. Access is conditional, temporary, and governed by contract terms that no individual or business would be expected to accept.
snippet: The European Citizens’ Initiative (ECI) on the right to resell digital games is on the agenda of a joint hearing by the European Parliament’s Internal Market and Consumer Protection (IMCO), Legal
